sourceMovie.title: Sleeping Beauty
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Sleeping Beauty for the same reason it broke through in 1959: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a animation rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Clyde Geronimi keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The fantasy and animation blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Shrek 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Sleeping Beauty clicked for you, Shrek 2 (2004) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and animation, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Beauty and the Beast
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Sleeping Beauty clicked for you, Beauty and the Beast (1991) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and animation, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Sleeping Beauty?
faq.answer: Try Shrek 2, Beauty and the Beast, Cinderella—each shares DNA with Sleeping Beauty while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Sleeping Beauty?
faq.answer: Sleeping Beauty (1959) is best described as Fantasy, Animation, Romance, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Sleeping Beauty worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.9/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
